After docking the robotic system, the surgeon isolates the femur's enteric artery at the pelvic brim, ensuring the hypogastric nerves are not damaged. The eye tree is isolated cleanly, two centimeters from its origin at the abdominal aorta.
The surgeon isolates the iliac artery, cleaning it two centimeters from its origin at the abdominal aorta.
The surgeon performs ligation using Hem-o-lok locking clips, followed by division of the isolated structure with monopolar scissors.

The surgeon secures the artery and proceeds with cranial dissection using a medial collateral approach, applying traction and counter traction in front of Gerota's fascia to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon secures the artery and proceeds with cranial dissection using a medial collateral approach. Traction and counter-traction are applied in front of Gerota's fascia, with gentle traction to minimize tissue trauma.
The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric vein at its origin near the lower border of the pancreas. Locking clips are applied to secure the vein, followed by division using a monopolar scissor.

The surgeon performs precise dissection laterally, cranially, and caudally using low voltage monopolar scissors, applying traction and counter traction to carefully separate tissue layers.

The surgeon performs dissection in front of the rotous fascia, minimizing the risk of trauma to the left ureter and glidal vessel. The robotic left hand applies careful traction to aid in the procedure.

Following complete mobilization of the left colon, the surgeon uses traction and counter-traction with robotic arms to focus on rectal dissection. The surgeon isolates the round ligaments and mobilizes the uterus from the back of the bladder to access the vasculature. A bipolar energy device in the left robotic hand ensures a complete vascular seal, creating a bloodless plane. The right vasculature is sealed using the bipolar energy source, and the tibiovarian vasculature is divided with a combination of bipolar energy and a monopolar scissor in the right robotic hand.

The surgeon isolates the round ligaments and mobilizes the body of the uterus from the posterior aspect of the bladder, providing access to the uterine vasculature from both superior and inferior positions.

The robotic surgical system uses a bipolar energy device in the left hand to seal the vasculature, creating a bloodless plane. The right vasculature is sealed with the bipolar energy source, followed by the division of the tibiovarian vasculature using a combination of bipolar energy and monopolar scissors in the right hand.
The surgeon exposes the right anterolateral aspect of the uterus, revealing the surrounding anatomical structures and tissue planes.

The surgeon exposes the right anterolateral aspect of the uterus, using a bipolar energy source and monopolar scissors to cut through the tissue while maintaining hemostasis. The uterine arteries are isolated and divided close to the specimen, ensuring the ureters are safeguarded on both sides.

The surgeon uses a bipolar energy source and monopolar scissors to carefully dissect tissue on the left pelvic side wall, ensuring hemostasis while safeguarding the nerves. The operative field remains bloodless as the rectal specimen is mobilized without further advancement.

The surgeon isolates and divides the uterine arteries on both sides, using a technique that keeps the dissection close to the specimen. This approach ensures the ureters are safeguarded during the procedure.

A swab on a stick is introduced through the vagina to highlight the back of the cervix, facilitating direct revision of the cervical wall using monoclonal diathermy. The rectal specimen is then mobilized further anterolaterally on both sides, with care taken to avoid excessive traction on the left pelvic hypogastric nerves.

The surgeon uses an endo-GIS taper to divide the Vector tube, followed by closing the defect in the posterior wall of the vagina and cervix with a running hemo-lock suture. The robotic instrumentation provides a static view, facilitating precise suturing with minimal arm clashes, resulting in a secure suture line.

The surgeon uses an endo-GIA stapler to divide the vector tube following complete mobilization. A defect in the posterior wall of the vagina and cervix is closed with a running hemo-lock suture. The robotic instruments provide a static view, allowing for precise suturing with minimal arm clashes, resulting in a secure suture line.
After the rectal division, the surgeon extracts the specimen and performs an end-to-end anastomosis using a circular stapling device, ensuring continuity of the rectal tissue.
